content:
For once in his academic career, Sol was actually studying. He sat criss-cross on the living room couch, posture deceptively relaxed, like he wasn’t fighting against the concept of academic responsibility. 

A thick textbook floated in front of him, held up by lazy threads of gold and silver magic that shimmered faintly whenever he focused. The title, *The Art of Transmutation*, was scrawled across the cover in sharp, elegant lettering.

Sol’s eyes tracked the pages, brows furrowed in concentration as he took notes in the notebook resting on his lap. His handwriting was surprisingly neat today; he even underlined important details twice, as if he were a real academic weapon.

On the table in front of him sat a cup of dark tea, steaming and half-forgotten. It rested on a single sheet of paper, with absent-minded sketches scattered across it. Tea stains bloomed at the paper’s corners like little bruises. Near the mug, other textbooks had been stacked in a chaotic pile: one had paint smears along the spine and was titled *Chromatic Expression & Advanced Brushwork*, another, a thicker book, had metallic lettering that read *Enchantment Theory*.

Nestled against Sol’s thigh, like an ancient cat who refused to be moved, was Bookie, snoozing so deeply his pages softly ruffled with each slow exhale.

“Mhm,” he murmured as he kept reading, tapping his pencil against the notebook. “Okay, *Slow* isn’t that complicated in theory. It just takes an annoying amount of concentration to maintain.

The floating textbook turned a page with a flick of his hand, the crinkle of the page crisp against the room's quiet. Sol leaned back into the couch cushions and narrowed his eyes when he read something he didn’t think was important. He scribbled it down anyway because, as much as he liked to pretend he didn’t care about his grades and academic performance, he *did* care a lot. Especially when he cast a spell in front of his peers, and it refused to bend to his will on the first try.

His gaze slid toward the open space in front of him, imagining how it would look if the spell manifested. A gentle frost lulling in the air, the faint sound of a slowing clock tick in his ears, and the slow motion of his opponents.

He returned his attention to the textbook and continued studying, determined to master the spell’s theory and execute it flawlessly.

<@206175579358232578>

Sol being ever so deeply enamored with his studies likely didn’t notice that Vornakir had walked not only past him while he worked in the common area, but also managed to make him a morning bowl of cereal. That much itself was odd, he never really made something so base and simple as a bowl of *cereal*. It was always a meal, fragrant and with the most fresh ingredients. And he always made enough for everyone. 

But today, hair a mess and none at all to bother fixing it, he strolled his princely ass through the common room in his pajamas and the most ostentatious part of his attire for the evening in the form of a lush robe over his shoulders and covering his chest. The mage hand he relied so heavily on carted the bowl in front of him so he could sit in the nice, plush arm chair just to the side of Sol’s couch. 

With his trusty mage hand holding the bowl aloft in front of him, he slowly spooned a couple of mouthfuls in to eat it quietly and properly to not break his friend’s concentration. Only when he broke his attention from envisioning the spell would he finally speak when Sol stared once more at the text book. 

“You’ll do well with that spell. The trick to *Slow*…” he said, pausing to spoon in another bite of cereal to chew and swallow before continuing, “At least for me anyway, the trick for mastering it came through music. Everything they would do during my concentration,” Another bite of the cereal, and properly waiting to swallow before speaking, “I imagined they were one long sustained note in a bar whereas I was allowed several notes. Or they’re allowed one note and all rests for the remainder.”

Nodding sagely to Sol, as if this trick was absolute gold handed to him on a platter - ancient wisdom passed down from mentor to student for centuries, he simply gave a tired smile to him. “I struggled with *Expeditious Retreat* for a while because I kept trying to use the same trick but the opposite - I found by mistake that it works better for *Haste*. But ultimately, I have faith in you. Everyone’s mind works differently.” 

Dark bags were under his eyes again, but not as bad as they had been in recent memory. They were returning though, and he did his best to hide them through his tired morning stupor. 

Dunking his spoon into the bowl to wet more pieces of the cereal with his milk, he canted a head to the side. “Never seen you take studying this seriously. I’m impressed.”

Sol didn’t initially look up as Vornakir spoke, but he was listening as he kept writing down notes in his notebook. The moment Vornakir started explaining his method behind casting the *Slow* spell, Sol’s writing hand slowed for a couple of seconds.

“Mhm,” he hummed while underlining something important.

When Vornakir finished, a smile tugged at the corner of Sol’s mouth. “That’s actually really smart,” he said, tapping his pencil against a page.

He glanced down at one of his own notes and added Vornakir’s music framing technique beneath it, before continuing.

“I don’t know if it’ll help me the same way, though,” Sol admitted, “it seems you’re an auditory learner and your brain is good with rhythm structure.” His pencil dotted the page, ending a sentence. “I’m a visual learner,” Sol continued. “The way I use my magic is by picturing the way the air could move, the way light could bend, the way a person’s expression could change when they realize they’ve been fooled.” 

He scribbled some more notes down. “Especially with illusion magic,” Sol said. “My whole thing is learning my opponent: what they believe in, what they’re afraid of, what they desire. And then,” his smile widened, “tailoring the world to it; making them see something *they* believe could actually happen.”

A beat of silence followed.  “But I will keep your advice in mind,” he added. “Thanks.”

He flipped the textbook page with a slight flick of his fingers, and at Vornakir’s comment about studying seriously, Sol let a laugh out through his nose.

“Well, I figured it was time I started applying myself after our exams.” He leaned back slightly into the couch cushions, “I did well enough off natural skill alone, but imagine what I could do if I actually *tried*.”

His pencil paused, and he grinned. “I guess Auri is finally rubbing off on me.”

Then he sighed dramatically, like someone carrying the world on his shoulders. “And honestly? I have a feeling my father will want to talk to me again soon, and I would prefer our fights stay over my fashion interests rather than my academics.”

He finished the sentence and finally lifted his gaze toward Vornakir, and the moment he did, his expression shifted. Something about the brightness behind his eyes changed as his attention sharpened in the way it did when he was clocking details other people missed. The robe, the pajamas, the hair, the faint shadowing under Vornakir’s eyes, dark enough that Sol knew he had tried to hide it, all things bizarre to see with Vornakir, but the most unusual thing was to see him eating cereal.

Sol’s concentration slipped, and the floating textbook softly plopped down onto the stack of other books on the table, the threads of gold and silver magic dissolving like mist. Sol stared at his friend for a second longer, then exhaled, his voice softening.

“Vornakir,” he said with no ounce of teasing. “We’re close enough now that I can tell you things, and I know you know it comes from a place of love.”

He paused, and Sol’s gaze flicked over Vornakir again, then back to his eyes. Then, with the most blunt honesty, Sol said: “You look like *shit*.” His brows lifted slightly, “What’s going on? You never let anyone catch you so un-put-together.”

Seemingly not bothered that his advice may or may not help - especially given that he prefaced with the same fact - Vornakir continued to munch away at his cereal in a somewhat discombobulated state. 

Licking his lips clean of any residue milk, he simply tilted his head owlishly at Sol. “Never thought to do it that way. I adapt most of my spells differently, some I process audiotorially. With others it helps to picture a panting. Some, a web that I weave from my fingertips. Hells, there are some spells I completely just wing it on. I innately feel the way the arcana flows and it’s almost like an extension of who I am.” 

A crack in his more “humble” appearance formed at the mention of Auri, a smile but nothing more for now. 

“No matter the reasoning,” he said as he paused his breakfast to briefly point his spoon toward Sol, “I’m proud of you. Not that my pride for others should mean anything, but I think it’s important you know you have support and someone out there is happy for you that you’re finding the will to do something that’ll only better yourself in the long run.” 

Short lived it was, his moment of friendly compliments because he paused again when Sol pointed out his state of being. He dropped the spoon into the bowl and waved his mage hand to move away a few inches so that he may produce a small pocket mirror to admire his face. If that was a good word for it.

Exhaling after a beat of silence, he rubbed his hand down his face in sheer annoyance. Not at Sol, but himself. “Honestly, the Archmage has me reeling with everything and I’m back into dueling so I have to try to fit that into my schedule. On top of all of that, I’m attempting to learn two new spells. *Slow*, which thankfully I mastered very quickly. I’m personally stuck on *Mirror Image*. It’s been keeping me up every night.”

Another beat of silence and he willed his mage hand to take his empty bowl across to the kitchen to clean it before he would dismiss it with the wave of his fingers. “I’ve only lost one duel since coming here and it’s come at one of the worst points of my mental. I attribute it to a lot of things, but Kael’s mastery over *Mirror Image* definitely played a heavy part in it. Losing to it is one thing, but failing to grasp the concept of using it myself is salt in the wound.”

Sol’s expression softened, and a smile appeared on his face when Vornakir said he was proud of him; Sol didn’t hear those words often, so it was a most welcome change.

“Thanks,” he smiled, all theatrics dropped in that moment.

When Vornakir talked about his struggles with *Mirror Image*, Sol leaned forward, elbows resting on his knees, notebook temporarily forgotten. He listened to his friend's conundrum, eyes tracking Vornakir's movements, the way frustration crept into his posture when he mentioned his duel with Kael.

When Vornakir finished, Sol flashed his warmest smile, reassurance shining behind it.

“Hey,” he said gently, “don’t worry too much about the spell.” He straightened his posture slightly, confidence settling over him like a cloak. “Illusion magic can be fickle and very tricky,” Sol continued, “but lucky for you, you’re friends with a *very* well-versed illusionist who’s more than willing to help you!” Sol's smile widened, unmistakably happy that he could finally teach someone in his area of expertise.

Sol shifted his weight, sitting up straighter on the couch, and an unmistakable spark filled his eyes.

“I used *Mirror Image* a lot as a kid,” he said casually. “It was actually one of the first spells I ever taught myself, so I can definitely guide you here.”

He paused, then tilted his head thoughtfully.

“Where most people struggle with illusion magic is that they think it’s about forcing people to see what *you* want them to.” 

He lifted one hand, fingers curling slightly as faint threads of gold and silver magic began to swirl around his wrist.

“But it’s actually the opposite,” he said with a smile. “It’s about coaxing them into seeing what *they* want to see and what *they* already believe *could* happen.”

His eyes flicked up to meet Vornakir’s. “If you try to be domineering with illusion magic, it will fight back against you, and your target will have an easier time resisting. But if you see the world through your target's eyes and use that to your advantage—”

Sol traced a smooth, somatic gesture through the air, and the space around him shimmered and distorted. Suddenly, Sol was no longer alone; three identical copies of him stepped into being: one lounged on the couch beside him, another stood behind Vornakir’s chair, and the last sat between Sol and Vornakir on the floor. Each mirrored Sol’s appearance and mannerisms with unsettling precision: breathing, shifting, and holding eye contact as naturally as the real one. Sol let his hand fall back to his lap, a cheeky smile on his face.

“—you’ll find,” he said, voice echoing and making it hard to place where he actually was, “that it’s much easier to trick them.”

Each of the Sols craned their head analytically, watching Vornakir like an owl.

“Now, how about you try?” they said in sync. “See the world through my eyes, and focus on what I would want to see.”

Vornakir straightened some with that same tired look as his head canted to one side. Usually too proud to even admit he struggled with something, the last several weeks weighed so much on him bit by bit that he’d opened up far more than usual in so many ways. Including accepting help. 

It helped that Vornakir saw the passion and excitement in his friend’s eyes, that he could teach someone in his most versed magic. He couldn’t help but smile at the spark. 

Mentally taking notes, he nodded along. The stained glass in his petaled eyes gleamed with understanding as Sol offered his advice. Despite that he’d been quite humbled and brought down from his pedestal more than once, the common denominator all along was his prodigious existence itself; many things came naturally to him, and many others he had to work for. Regardless if it were natural or not though, mastery came easier to him than most. A fact that wasn’t lost on him or taken for granted as of late.

“I see. And in some manner, it has to feel natural. It has to be as real as me or it’ll be easy to break through. Don’t *force* you to see more of me…but *allow* you to see more of me, so to speak? As if I’m pulling back the curtains on something that’s we both believe has been true and been there all along.” It wasn’t verbatim for Sol’s advice but the same spirit was there. 

He definitely learned his spells differently each time, the mental chalkboard of his spell book must be a horrific mess that only he could ever hope to decipher. 

Mimicking Sol’s somatic gestures but in his own way - it was the same at its core, but each movement was disciplined and like a bolt of lightning as all his spells were. Quick, efficient, heavy with power but flowing freely to its target. 

Not yet a master, he did not conjure three extra flawless Vornakir clones. But two, which was much better than the singular one he’d been stuck with since beginning his studying of the spell, appeared in a seamless golden fog as if they stepped out of a heavenly radiant light and into view as naturally as if they’d been there all along. 

One leaned itself against the wall and the other leaned over the back of Vornakir’s chair with a curious expression. 

In unison, they all cursed. “Fucking hells…I mean two is fine but…how can I add another? I barely managed one before.”

Sol had a proud smile plastered across his face, the kind that only showed up when a teacher saw a student’s progress.

“See?” he said, gesturing toward Vornakir and his two copies. “It’s huge progress to summon two copies when you were originally stuck at one! You should be proud of yourself; that means your spell’s foundation is there!”

He rose from the couch in one smooth motion, energized by the spellwork in front of him.

“The thing about *Mirror Image* is the secret to fully casting the spell is hidden in the name,” Sol continued. “Yes, part of the spell is mirroring what your opponent wants to see into the space around you,” he said while lifting one finger. 

Then he lifted a second finger beside it. “But the other half?” He tapped his fingers to his chest. “Is mirroring *yourself* outward.”

Sol turned and gestured toward his copies.

“For me,” he said, pointing to the illusion seated on the couch, “it’s jubilation.”

The copy immediately brightened, flashing Vornakir a wide, radiant smile and letting out a loud, playful laugh, as if everything in the world were made of sugar and rainbows.

He pointed next to the illusion standing just behind Vornakir’s chair, posture straight, gaze observant and unreadable.

“Insight.”

The copy’s eyes tracked Vornakir and his duplicates with quiet focus, head craning like a cat as if analyzing everything at once.

Then Sol gestured toward the third illusion, who had initially been sitting on the floor, but now had stood up, wandered over, and attempted to hug one of Vornakir’s mirror images. Its arms phased straight through the clone, and the illusion’s shoulders slumped, looking genuinely sad.

Sol’s expression softened. “And love.”

A faint pastel pink shimmer brushed the illusion’s irises as it glanced back toward Sol like it wasn’t sure what it had done wrong.

Sol let the moment breathe before continuing.

“It’s a fifty-fifty spell,” he said. “Half understanding how your opponent thinks. Half understanding yourself,” he paused for a second, “and not just the pretty parts,” Sol added. “The good. The bad. The morally grey. The things you’re proud of. The things you wish would disappear.”

He gave an encouraging smile.

“The more honest you are about who you are, the more reflections you’ll be able to make. 

“The more you neglect reflecting your true self, the fewer copies you’ll manage.” He gestured back to Vornakir’s two duplicates. “And the less you understand your target, the less convincing your copies will be.”

He smiled again. “Makes sense, right? It’s like a tango; if one person messes up, both of you fall on your ass!” He and the clones laughed loudly at his comparison.

Sol crossed his arms loosely, looking at Vornakir encouragingly.

“So, focus on the traits that make Vornakir, *Vornakir*,” he gave a slight nod, “and try again.”

Thankful that it began to click somewhat, at least, he dispelled the magic to regroup his thoughts so he could try again after the next round of advice. 

Vornakir lifted his hand and rubbed at the back of his neck with furrowed brow, determined now to not fail again. And so he listened. He watched each individual image of Sol as he explained through the improvement process. 

Like a predator observing prey from a distance he logged every minute detail to memory. The way his jubilation and joy rose with radiance and pure euphoria in Aasimar form. The way his insight and pragmatic thinking rivaled his own expression, watching him in return with resolve and focus. And finally as the third one got too familiar with one of his to no avail, embodying the love Sol felt for all he held dear. 

Fundamentally it was simple. His spellwork was precise and perfect, it was the energy and thought behind the weave itself that strained his mastery of *Mirror Image*.

What did Vornakir think in and of himself. What was the good, what was the bad that he and all the others could find in him? What were his core traits that could each be mirrored outward from him, projecting his truest self for others to see. 

More methodical, this time as he performed the spell it was slower. Meticulously brushed like an artist painting with deliberate and premeditated, the strokes of auric energy wove individual images one after the other instead of all at once seamlessly. Of course, this method of casting would be useless in a high stakes moment as it was often needed in but getting the absolute fundamentals and precise facets of the spell mastered would lead to conjuring them all at the same time quickly in the future.

First sauntered out of thin air the most anticipated and expected Vornakir; Pride. If the first Sin that entered the heart of man was given form, it stood before Sol today. Back straight, chin lifted, eyes angled down so that even someone of greater stature was looked down upon somehow. His very aura was the color of the sin he represented, the way he appraised everyone and everything around him with an obvious flair of being greater than it all was obscenely *obnoxious* to behold yet one strained to look away. He commanded the room. 

Second came another heavy hitter - one who rivaled Pride in how pure its fractal personality shone through. His expression was cold and lifeless, indifferent to anything and everything that crossed it as it slumped into its own arm chair and gazed its hollow eyes into nothing. Deep rooted, unresolved and stitched together with a hope and a prayer sat what could only be the embodiment of Vornakir’s sepulchral Sorrow. Burned out, laden with expectation and a never ending spiral of grief and ache.

However, just as a third one began to materialize it halted. Not out of Vornakir’s lack of talent or trying but he halted the spell himself. Staring at the empty space where the third should have materialized - clearly he’d stopped for a reason.

Sol watched as the two figures emerged from Vornakir. He could tell who they were before Vornakir said anything: pride and sorrow. Pride’s feelings of superiority were evident in its posh posture, and Sol could feel how it had a presence that it never questioned. Sorrow looked empty and tired in a way that went beyond sleep rectifying.

Then Sol noticed it: the hesitation written along Vornakir’s face, the way the space for a third copy never filled, the way Vornakir had stopped casting in the middle of the spell.

Sol’s illusions reacted before he consciously told them to. Jubilation dissolved first, bursting into motes of warm gold magic, laughter fading away. Insight dissipated next into a thin line of cerulean light. Love was the last to go; it was still lingering where it had tried to hug Vornakir’s copy, but after a few seconds, it broke apart into pale pink mist that slowly drifted to the floor like snow.

The room was quieter without them around. Sol took a slow breath as he analyzed the scene before him; he wanted to piece everything together before speaking. Pride made sense, as did sorrow, but what was missing? Shame? Vulnerability? Fear? Was it something Vornakir wasn’t ready to look at yet, or something he didn’t want Sol to see?

Sol didn’t know, so he didn’t assume anything. Instead, he crossed the space between them and sat on the floor in front of Vornakir, cross-legged, resting his arms loosely on his knees, and offering his friend a friendly smile.

“You know,” Sol said lightly, trying to ease the weight of the situation, “Redd and I joke that I’m the resident group therapist.” He lightly laughed. “So, therapy is now in session,” he said with a slight joking tone.

Then, his voice became more sincere: “I’ve been friends with you long enough to know when something’s bothering you.” He put a finger against his temple. “I don’t need magic to know that.” 

Sol craned his head a bit and smiled. “I don’t expect you to tell me anything you’re not ready to,” he said gently. “But I can offer you the limited life advice I have.”

Sol held Vornakir’s gaze for a long moment before he sighed. “Facing yourself is scary,” he said with a quiet, somber voice. “Especially the parts you don’t want others to see.”

He shifted where he sat, fingers idly brushing against the floor as he continued to speak.

“We spend so much of our lives curating different personas to show different people: deciding which pieces of us are acceptable, which ones are useful, which ones are impressive.” He let out a small huff. “Then we shove the undesirable pieces into a box and pretend they don’t exist, and that can make you feel broken or like you’re failing yourself.”

Sol glanced once more at Vornakir’s illusions and the space where the third should’ve been.

“But I just want you to know, you’re not broken, Vornakir,” he said gently, “and you’re not failing. I just think you haven’t been able to decide if it’s okay for others to see a certain part of you yet.”

A long pause filled the room until Sol lifted his hand, and his familiar golden-silver magic swirled around his wrist again. In the blink of an eye, Sol’s mirror images appeared again around him, but these weren’t the same ones from before.

To his left stood an angry Sol: magic violently crackled around it, its fists clenched, and its eyes blazed with fury, as if it were about to punch through the nearest window. Sol’s hot-headed temper: the part of him that acted first and asked questions later.

To his right was a Sol with wide eyes, looking much smaller in stature due to its hunched shoulders. It tried, very unsuccessfully, to duck underneath the table, as if hiding might make the world forget it existed. Sol’s fear: of losing people, of pushing things too far, of becoming someone who hurt the ones he loved.

Finally, behind him, a third Sol lingered in the shadow. It watched Vornakir with a malicious glare, but if Vornakir looked close enough, he’d see a tight, aching expression lingering behind the disdain on its face. Sol had brought his jealousy out to play; it was the part of him that compared himself to his friends and wondered why things seemed to come so naturally for them but not to him. This was the ugliest part of himself he buried deep within.

Sol looked at Vornakir. “We all have demons we want to hide,” he said softly, gesturing around to his copies. “But when you bring them into the light, they aren’t so scary anymore.”

He flicked his fingers again, and golden magic swirled around his illusions. The angry Sol unclenched its fists, and the tension in its posture melted into buoyant energy. It transformed seamlessly into jubilation, bursting into bright laughter and smiles.

The frightened Sol slowly straightened its shoulders and stopped trembling. Its wide-eyed panic transformed into calm awareness, and insight returned, observant and thoughtful.

Lastly, the malice and ache that twisted jealousy’s face softened it into yearning, then into care, and finally into compassion. The illusion stepped forward and hugged Sol from behind; love returned, ready to make everyone feel valued.

Sol glanced at his illusions, then returned his gaze to Vornakir. “My core parts never change,” Sol said, “they’re the beautiful and ugly faces of the same coin.”

He smiled. “My temper is just jubilation when it hits a wall and has nowhere to go. My fear is what happens when my insight doesn’t know what’s coming. My jealousy?” He huffed a tiny laugh. “That’s just love twisted up by fear and yearning.”

He met Vornakir’s eyes again, with a sincere look. “I know it’s hard to reflect on parts of yourself you think are undesirable, but when you accept them for who they are,” he gestured toward jubilation, insight, and love, “they don’t have to stay that way.” He smiled warmly at his friend. “And they can blossom into something beautiful.”

He dismissed the illusions again with a snap of his fingers, and he looked back at Vornakir. “So Vornakir, what do you think your third piece is trying to be?”

Both Pride and Sorrow, two sides of the same coin where Vornakir was concerned, fixed him with an incredulous stare as he halted the spell’s completion. 

Fixated for a distraction, he watched how Sol’s illusions dissolved one by one. The silence that hung between them spread thinner and thinner by the moment as Sol analyzed what happened. It was clear by the way Vornakir’s body language spoke that the reason he didn’t show it was likely a mixture of both not wanting to see it humanized before him and not sure if he was ready to be that vulnerable with Sol yet.

Scoffing as his friend settled in front of him on the floor cross legged, mostly at the concept of it being some impromptu therapy session. Trying to diffuse the mood the best he could, he managed a small smile. “Last time I had to have therapy it made me fall behind Caspian in spell-duels.” 

Despite that, he listened to Sol. He didn’t interrupt, merely absorbed it all - without making eye contact the entire time. His eyes remained now on the empty space where his third image was supposed to manifest. The place where empty potential remained in the form of an auric mist. 

Just when he thought he had something witty or appropriate to say to give in rebuttal, Sol kept going and provided a demonstration. Finally he took his stare off the unlimited potential space and gave his attention to each of the copies that now mirrored Sol’s darker sides. More silence stretched past when he asked that question, a long beat that almost stretched long enough to make things awkward. 

Pride twisted into Shame. He no longer stood at the center of attention but slid out of view to hide himself away from the world. Lips parted from the real V, and he spoke at last. “Do you know who I am, Sol…? Who I really am?” He doubted that Sol did, but he gave him a beat to answer if he wanted. Regardless of what he said, V continued on.

“Everyone knows by now I’m one of the few that may become the Sovereign of Ebonspire. But it goes deeper than that. Ebonspire is the Circle I’m from - it’s not the only one in the Barricade, not even the most powerful. But it’s still important as home to The Black Bloom. I’ll spare you the history of why  we’re taught at a young age that Pride is the most important thing we can have. It’s already a given just being from there that I’ll have a deep rooted sense of it.”

Clearing his throat, he watched Sorrow shift into pure elation out of the corners of his eyes. “And it’s a given that I’m to be even more prideful being a Scaleborn *and* that my mother is the current Sovereign. But deeper than that, my ancestors go back so far that one of them was one of the original Founding Elders of Ebonspire. My last name alone carries more weight and expectation than entire bloodlines back home.”

Rubbing absently at the back of his neck he turned to watch the empty space again and gestured with a nod toward it for Sol to watch as the third image walked out of the mist. 

Crippling Fear? Or was it Desperation? It shared a core trait with Vornakir all his friends would recognize immediately. Constant vigilance, watching every corner. But it looked as if it wanted to collapse in on itself like a dying star. Every trace of radiance and pride and elation was wiped from him.

“Expectation has always been heavy on me. The price of failure has always been death. Not just during my trial but all throughout my life. Rarely have I ever been allowed to make my own decisions, and the few I can make are either illusion of choice or will result in death if I choose incorrectly. From the moment I could walk, death has followed me around every corner. Sometimes in the form of assassins, sometimes in the form of a lesson where success resulted in pride and failure in death.”

Each illusion of Vornakir shimmered as hi concentration began to falter. All it would take was a glance at V to see why - tears welled at his eyes and threatened to spill out. But as composed as he could be, his voice barely held together, he exposed the root of his fear even deeper.

“When I lost my arm, I almost died. Everyone knows that. And some of you know I saw a woman that I can only assume is some…avatar guarding the afterlife, or representing death. It was the first time in my life that I was allowed to choose. I could have gone on, I could have found peace and been released of all of this shit. But for the first time in my life, making the choice that led to life resulted in me wondering if I should have chosen the other road. I’m going to die someday, that much is a promise. I’ll outlive many people I care about. And since my life is a story that’s already mostly written by everyone else’s pen, my death is the thing I could have had control over. I won’t be remembered for my life. They’ll just begin writing someone else’s when it’s over. But my death? That *could* mean something. What good is my story if it ends at the hands of some unnamed assassin, or my cousin who wants to be Sovereign?”

And for a moment, perhaps it may have made sense why Vornakir wanted the idea of being an Archmage. A life of freedom, a life of power over his own life and death. An end to his story without a shameful or worthless death.

A small frown appeared on Sol's face when he noticed the tears in Vornakir’s eyes; he hadn’t thought the drow was capable of crying, much less crying *in front* of other people. Sol waved his hand, and silver magic curled through the air like a gentle current. From the direction of Sol’s room, a small, elegant tissue box drifted into the common space; the tissues looked like they had a soft, cloudlike texture. The box hovered beside Vornakir’s arm, waiting to be used if he needed it.

Sol exhaled slowly and set both his palms against the floor to ground himself before he spoke. When he lifted his gaze to Vornakir, his eyes were full of sympathy and understanding.

“Believe it or not,” Sol began, “I get the whole *‘illusion of choice’* thing.” He let the words sit between them for a second. “After my father’s businesses closed,” Sol continued, “and after Lucien got sick, it was like the ground vanished out from under me.” 

Sol’s fingers curled slightly against the floor. “Suddenly, I didn’t get to decide things for myself anymore.” His voice began to take on a tinge of sadness. “I couldn’t decide who I could be friends with, what tutors I was allowed to have, what magic I could study, what hobbies were deemed acceptable.” A small, bitter huff escaped him. “My father took control of my whole life, and gods forbid I tried to rebel and choose something for myself for once, he’d make sure I face severe consequences.”

He briefly glanced down at the floor, then back to Vornakir. “I know my situation isn’t on the same scale as yours,” Sol said. “And I know it’s not nearly as lethal, but I understand what it feels like when someone else writes your story for you.”

He paused for a second, looking deeply into Vornakir’s ochre eyes. “I understand the weight of expectations, too.”

Sol lifted one hand, and soft golden-silver light flared, shifting the space around, as illusions bloomed around them. None of them were extravagant or heroic like one would expect Sol to conjure, in fact they were all pretty downright depressing: Sol drowning beneath a roaring cascade of water, dragged down during Aurum’s second trial by a massive water elemental; Sol collapsing as poison burned through his veins in the fight against Velven; Sol stumbling backward in frustration as undead ignored his commands during the battle with the Vialphage and Aelira, charm magic sliding uselessly off rotting flesh.

Failure after failure. Defeat after defeat.

Sol swallowed. “I’m supposed to be this powerful illusionist and prodigy enchanter; this cornerstone of the group.” His voice wavered, sadness welling up inside him. “But the truth is, I feel useless in combat.”

The illusions hung heavy in the air like haunting specters. “I’m always the first one down, the first one who needs to be protected, the first one who has to retreat.” He exhaled quietly. “Sometimes I think you all would be better off without me there.”

The illusions shattered into drifting motes of light, and two familiar figures flickered into existence beside Sol: Fear and Jealousy. The smaller, frightened Sol hovered close to the floor again as the other lingered behind him, tensely watching Vornakir.

Sol looked down at the floor again. “Expectations make me work extremely hard because I’m terrified that if I’m not good enough, I’m going to lose everyone I care about.” 

He breathed in sharply. “And they make me jealous of all of you: I’m not strong like Caspian, I can’t shift into anything I want to be like Redd, I’m not smart or strategic like Auri, and I’m not naturally adept at magic like you. You all can hold yourselves together in fights, while I feel like I’m dead weight.”

He looked up at Vornakir, sorrow and a touch of embarrassment painted all across his features. Sol realized he was trauma dumping again, and he took in a deep breath. His shoulders loosened, and Fear and Jealousy faded away, leaving the two of them alone again.

Sol stared at the floor for a moment before speaking again. “I don’t know how to fix any of that yet,” he admitted, “but I understand you more than I thought I did. It feels like I’m looking in a mirror sometimes when I talk to you.”

Sol shifted and leaned back against the base of Vornakir’s chair, gaze drifting up toward the ceiling to avoid eye contact with Vornakir, clearly trying to keep himself from crying and adding more emotional fuel to the fire.

His voice came out soft and genuine when he next spoke. “For what it’s worth,” he said quietly, “I’m glad you chose not to cross over.” A beat of silence filled the space. “Even though you drive me insane sometimes,” a faint, warm smile tugged at his mouth, “I’d really miss you if you were gone.”

He fell silent after that, and in that quiet, Sol’s magic stirred. It began as a gentle warmth spreading across his skin, like standing in the last light of day. Then color followed as a soft haze bled out in slow, lazy curls: pale pinks melted into warm peach, lavender threaded through gold. The magic continued to billow and coalesce until it formed a fluffy, cotton-candy-colored, cloudlike mist that pooled around them. It covered the floor, drifted around Sol’s shoulders, and rose upwards in feather-like plumes.

It didn’t look like Sol was intentionally casting a spell, but an inviting presence was surrounding them. If Vornakir allowed himself to lean into it, the sensation would wrap around him like invisible arms, and steady and grounding warmth would envelop him like a heated blanket. It would feel like the kind of warmth that only comes around when you feel like you belong, like when you feel like family. 

Whether Sol meant to reveal it or not, Vornakir would know that Sol viewed him as part of his family if he acknowledged the magical aura around them.

Sol didn’t seem to notice any of what was happening around him. He stayed where he was, back against the chair, eyes fixed on the ceiling, breathing slow and even, as his magic acted with a mind of its own.

Not so apprehensively but more in realization he’d let the wall down further than he intended would’ve side eye the box of tissues. It was true that he was *capable* of shedding tears but the concept of allowing another to see it likely shamed him deeper than he cared to admit at the moment. Nonetheless, he extended his hand to take a couple to primarily “fix his face”. Wiping away tears, dabbing under his nostrils just in case, and then using another to wipe at his eyes again to ensure they were dry. 

Pity wasn’t something he often wanted, nor was sympathy but for some reason today it was okay to him. The wall was already breached, the truth was already out and no reason remained to reject basic empathy. 

Especially not Sol’s. He was learning quickly despite how thick those walls typically were, oftentimes Sol could see the colors seeping through the crack. Vornakir closed his eyes, allowing himself the moment to compose his presentation once again while simultaneously listening to the new story. 

That’s when once again he couldn’t help but agree and see those parallels. In ways it was comforting to know someone knew at least a fraction of what he’d gone through. Whether it was a small or large fraction mattered not, but it sounded like it was more than base understanding. 

“You know, I don’t think my situation is on a greater scale in honesty. I know I say it a lot about how nobody can grasp it but I think it’s just…my wanting to feel more significant than I really am. We all experience things differently; What’s heavy to me may not be to you and vice versa. And I can tell it weighs heavily on you.” He admitted with the faintest weak smile but it was genuine as he opened his eyes and laid them on Sol. 

A breathless chuckle came at the illusion and he shook his head at each image. “Not dismissing your concerns, but you should look at these situations in a new light.” He began - each time an image produced itself he gestured toward it. “Honestly before you arrived, I was sort of in your position. It’s…sad, but the truth. I’ve learned maybe I shouldn’t weigh my contributions in my failures but in my success. Think about all the times you were directly the reason we survived. Healing us, inspiring us, motivating us. Taking massive creatures out of a fight even if for only moments. We all have a role to play. None of us could replace the others.” 

Carefully, he pushed up out of the chair and strolled around to lower himself as to sit at Sol’s side. He didn’t stir him, or disturb him physically yet. Instead he would simply sit there, mimicking the pose and closing his eyes to let the warmth rest over his shoulders. The significance wasn’t lost on him. The sensation was felt thoroughly, and the weight was comforting. 

“Thanks.” He murmured and if Sol would let him, he’d put his arm over Sol’s shoulder for a brief side hug. “Honestly, you don’t know how much this helps. And for what it’s worth…I don’t think we’d be better without you. I especially wouldn’t. You know, growing up my siblings and I never could be super close. We were *close* but it never felt like they would let me in fully. Or maybe I wouldn’t let them in, to keep them safe. I never wanted anything of my life to affect them.” 

A beat of silence and a smile against his own will as he turned his head to the side and opened his eyes toward his friend. “But I feel a lot of that bond I yearned to have with them with you.” 

He let Sol interpret that however he wanted but the aura he began to radiate fluttered into a soft silver mist. Something you might imagine hovering over a moonlit pool of crystalline water. An emotional state Sol would know from V translated to a form of love. One reserved for people that hold significance to him in some way. Irreplaceably.

Sol didn’t resist when Vornakir’s arm came around his shoulders; he leaned into it and tipped his head, resting comfortably against Vornakir’s shoulder. He let out a slow, satisfied breath, the kind that only came when you felt comfortable and safe. Hugs had always been his favorite form of affection; there was something about them that words couldn’t relay.

For a few seconds, he just let himself exist there: warmth at his side, soft sunset haze faintly lingering around. When he finally spoke, his voice was light and quiet.

“I hadn’t really thought my stalling tactics were that helpful,” he admitted with a small huff of amusement, “but I suppose we all don’t need to fell our opponents to be useful.” He shifted slightly so he could look at Vornakir. “We all have our roles,” he added, “Sometimes I just forget that mine isn’t supposed to look like yours.” A faint grin appeared on his face. “I’ll try to look at it from that perspective from now on.”

His smile softened when Vornakir talked about his relationship with his siblings. “I’m really close with Lucien,” Sol said. “He’s always been my anchor.” He curled his fingers absently around his sleeve cuffs. “I only ever had one sibling growing up, so I can’t really relate to what it’s like to grow up with more than one.” He tilted his head thoughtfully. “But I do have Caspian now.”

A smile brightened his face. “It’s funny, Caspian and Lucien are so similar, yet also so incredibly different.” He glanced down at his hands. “They’re both very protective, ridiculously intelligent, and willing to burn the world down for the people they care about.” He quietly laughed. “But Caspian has this fiery, relentless passion about everything he does, and it’s loud in the best ways possible.” A softer tone entered his voice. “Lucien, though, he’s tranquil and quiet; he’s the kind of person who moves through life like a steady stream and adapts to whatever is in his way.”

Sol’s gaze lifted, then shifted to Vornakir. “And then there’s you.” He smiled wider. “You’re so different from both of them, but you have small similarities.” He turned his face to look at him properly. “You’re also terrifyingly smart, but moreso about how you move through the world; you think ten steps ahead even when you don’t have to. And you have so much pride,” he nudged Vornakir lightly with his shoulder, “not that it’s bad; it’s the type the other two don’t really have. The kind that says you *know* who you are, even when you’re struggling.”

As Vornakir’s silver aura began to rise like moonlight over still water, Sol felt it brush against him. The same sense of anchoring he felt around Lucien was present now.

Sol’s expression softened, and he briefly inhaled. “You know,” he said, eyes drifting forward thoughtfully, “I came to Spark-Oath with only one brother,” he pressed his head a little more onto Vornakir’s shoulder, “now, after I graduate,” he glanced up, warmth in his gaze, “I get to leave with three.”

Sol didn’t speak the next part aloud; instead, in a soft, playful, and sincere tone, he let the thought drift across the psychic thread that connected him to other people. 

*Family gets to call me Sunni.*

The sunset haze around him warmed in response, and he stayed there, head resting against Vornakir’s shoulder.
